front: What is Just In Time compilation? (JIT)
back: Compilation during runtime. Combines the speed of compiled code with the flexibility of interpretation.

front: What is the downside of JIT?
back: There is a delay in the initial execution

front: What is Static Analysis?
back: Analysis of the source code without executing the program

front: When is a function computable?
back: When there exists a program which computes it.

front: Which is used more in practice, compilation or interpretation?
back: A combination of both.

front: What is partial evaluation?
back: A partial evaluator is like a across between an interpreter and a compiler. The partial evaluator will take input and only evaluate the parts of the program the depend on those inputs.

front: What is an interpreter?
back: Program that interprets instructions. Fetch-decode-execute. When a language is interpreted, the program runs from the interpreter.

front: What is the difference between a compiler and an interpreter?
back: A compiler is faster, but less flexible, and an interpreter is slower but more flexible. Compilers will not compile if there is an error in the code, while an interpreter will stop if there is a code. Interpreters also don't generate executables

front: How does a partial evaluator work?
back: It reads some source code, then builds an abstract syntax tree from which it reads some of the inputs to the program and analyses the program to see when the inputs provided are used in the program.

front: What is an abstract machine?
back: An abstract machine is a set of data structures and algorithms that can store and run programs. An abstraction of a physical computer.

front: What is the name of the technique that consists of evaluating a program against an input to produce a program that is more efficient than the original
back: Partial Evaluation

front: What is a compiler?
back: A compiler is a program that is used for translation to target machine code. A compiler directly translates to the machine code and generates an intermediate file, which acts as an executable that you can run.

front: What is the first step of an interpreter’s execution cycle?
back: Fetching the next instruction to execute from memory

front: When were low-level languages used to program the first computers?
What is the program that translates assembly to machine code called?
back: Assembler

front: What is OpCode?
back: A unique code which identifies one of the primitive operations defined by the machine’s hardware

front: The operations for processing primitive data are implemented by what?
back: ALU (Arithmetic-logic unit)

front: What was the most famous computer on which microprogramming was used?
back: IBM 360

front: What is the term used for simulation using microprogramming?
back: Emulation

front: In real language implementations, is compilation or interpreation usually used?
back: Both elements are almost always present.

front: What kind of machine is the Java Virtual Machine and its bytecode language an example of?
back: An intermediate machine

front: What happens in the Design of Language stage?
back: Bindings between primitive constants, types and operations of the language are defined (for example, + indicates addition, and int denotes the type of integers etc.)

front: Does there exist programs which are not computable?
back: The undecidability of the halting problem assures us that there exist functions which are not computable.

front: How can a program take another program as an input and determine if it terminates?
back: It can't.

front: What is the halting problem?
back: The halting problem is a problem which states that no program can be written to predict whether or not any other program halts after a finite number of steps.

front: Where is a declaration local to a block visible?
back: That block and all the blocks nested within it

front: When does the environment change?
back: On the entry and exit of a block.

front: What are Denotable Objects?
back:
Objects to which a name can be given.
Here is a non-exhaustive list: 

  • Objects whose names are defined by the user: Variables, formal parameters, procedures, user-defined types, labels, modules, user-defined constants, exceptions
  • Objects whose names are defined by the programming languge: primitive types, primitive operations, predefined constants

front: When are changes in the environment produced?
back: At entry and exit from a block

front: Which programming languages are turing complete (meaning they can compute the same set of functions as those computed by a Turing Machine)?
back: Every general purpose programming language

front:
Take the following example: (c-like)
{
    int a = 2; 
    {...}
}
To who is a visible?
back: The block it has been declared in, as well as all blocks listed within it, unless there is a new declaration of the same name in that same block.

front: If we have a variable, integer a = 10, do we know whether the name and the object are the same?
back: The name and the object they denote are never the same. No matter if it's a function or a variable or anything else.

front: What is the difference between static and lexical scope?
back: They are two words for the same thing

front: What are names?
back:
Sequence of characters used to denote something. E.g. 
int name = 2
const pi = 3.14

front: Which functionality manages the allocation of memory for programs and for data?
back: Interpreter

front: A stack uses which scheme, LIFO, FIFO or FILO?
back: LIFO

front: What is the difference in an activation record for a procedure and for a function?
back: For functions the activation record must also keep tabs on the memory location in which the function stores its return value.

front: A block A is entered, then a block B is entered, with the LIFO scheme, which block do we leave first?
What is the LIFO scheme?
back: Last in first out.

front: Is memory management a function of a compiler or interpreter associated with an abstract machine?
back: Interpreter

front: When is static memory management performed and by what?
back: By the compiler before execution starts

front: How is the runtime management of the system stack implemented?
back: By code fragments which the compiler or interpreter inserts immediately before and after the call to a procedure or before the start and after the end of a block

front: What is the memory space allocated on the stack, dedicated to an in-line block or to an activation of a procedure called?
back: The activation record or frame.

front: What does the structure of a generic activation record for an in-line block hold?
back: Dynamic chain pointer, local variables and intermediate results

front: What are intermediate results in an in-line block of an activation record?
back: Results which are only needed for something else. For example b = (a + x) / (x + y), a+x and x+y will be calculated and stored before they are divided (depending on the compiler).

front: What is a procedure?
back: An independent code module that fulfills some concrete task and is referenced within a larger body of source code

front: Where does statically allocated memory objects reside?
back: In a fixed zone of memory which is determined by the compiler, and they remain there for the entire duration of the program's execution.

front: Are constants, object code and global variables usually statically or dynamically allocated?
back: Statically

front: What does the dynamic chain pointer store? (recall: in-line block of a generic activation record)
back: A pointer to the previous activation record on the stack (or to the last activation record created).

front: What does it mean that we are using a stack for memory purposes?
back: We use them to push activation records or frames.

front: Why do we need memory?
back: To store the state of the program

front: Why do we need a stack for dynamic memory, instead of just dumping everything in memory?
back: We need it for recursion. With recursion we cannot allocate all possible blocks, but we can with a stack.

front: How do we implement static scoping?
back: We are using the static chain pointer. The compiler adds this pointer to the previous "father" block, which contains the known local variable.

front: What is an expression?
back: A syntactic entity whose evaluation yields a value or does not end, in which case the expression is undefined. There are three main notations, infix, prefix and postfix.

front: What are side effects?
back: Changing the state of computation without returning a value

front: When was if then else introduced?
back: In ALGOL 60

front: Why do we not use goto anymore?
back: It makes the code harder to read

front: What kinds of data structures are we using in memory management ?
back: Stack and the heap

front: What is call by result?
back: Opposite of value. The value of the result is returned and modified, but is not a reference. foo(result y) {y + 1}, then y would be y + 1 from here on.

front: Why do we care about tail recursion?
back: It's faster

front: What happens to the variable inside of a function using call by result after the function is done running?
back: It is destroyed (removed from the stack)

front: Why do we care about expressions?
back: Because we want to compute stuff

front: What are the two main ways that parameters are passed?
back: By value and by reference

front: You can call by value and by reference, but what else?
back: Constant, Result, name and value-result

front: Can you do lazy evaluation if you evaluate right-to-left?
back: No

front: What is type equivalence by name?
back: Two types are equivalent if they have the same name. Used in Pascal, Ada and Java

front: Type LessThan = 0..9 is an example of which type?
back: Intervals

front: What is a type?
back: A collection of values (homogeneous and effectively represented (can be visualized)) equipped with a set of operations to manipulate these values.

front: What defines what is a type and what is not a type?
back: The programming language.

front: When were enumerations introduced?
back: In Pascal

front: What is a scalar type?
back: A type where values are not composed of aggregations of other values (e.g. boolean, chars).

front: What are the pros of call by value?
back: You don't have to worry about side-effects.

front: How many categories of types are there?
back: Two, scalar types (or simple types) and composite types.

front: When are two types equivalent?
back: If each object of type T1 is also an object of type T2 and vice versa. T1 is compatible with T2 if an object of T1 can appear where a T2 would be expected.

front: When were intervals (subranges) introduced?
back: In Pascal

front: What is the particularity of call by constant?
back: It's read only, otherwise it works like call by reference.

front: How many ways of passing parameters are there?
back: 6, Call by value, reference, result, result-value, constant and name

front: What are composite types?
back: Types obtained by combining other types with appropriate constructors (e.g. string, pointer, struct).

front: What are the pros of call by reference?
back: You don't have to copy anything

front: Are enumerations ordered or unordered?
back: Ordered

front: What is the difference between an exception and an error?
back: An Error "indicates serious problems that a reasonable application should not try to catch." An Exception "indicates conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch."

front: What is the idea of finally?
back: To have a block that will be executed no matter what in a try block.

front: What was the first high-level programming language designed for computers? (Not implemented)
back: Plankalkül (Plan Calculus) in 1942-1945 by Konrad Zuse

front: Which programming language introduced the use of blocks?
back: Algol60

front: Why is lisp significant?
back: It was the first functional programming language, as well as the only programming language for artificial intelligence for years.

front: Which was the first effectively implemented high-level programming language?
back: Fortran in 1956

front: What was the first programming language?
back: Fortran

front: When did Charles Babbage invent the Analytical Engine?
What are Lisps sucessors?
back: Miranda, ML, Haskell

front: What is (was) the use-case of cobol?
back: Business-oriented computations. It has a very strict program organization and poor control structures.

front: Who invented the Analytical Engine?
back: Charles Babbage

front: What programming style (Imperative, Functional, Declarative, Functional Logic) is Prolog?
back: Declarative

front: What is Java originally designed for?
back: Internet programming, but is a general-purpose language.

front: What did fortran introduce?
back:
• Variables
  • Loops
  • Procedures
  • Statement labels
  • Much more

front: Where would Fortran still be used?
back: In engineering applications that require much array manipulation

front: What is significant about basic?
back:
It's the first programming language for personal computers. 
It's also designed to be easy to learn (however not anymore).

front: What did Simula 67 introduce?
back: Object Orientation: classes and encapsulation

front: What was the predecessor of Smalltalk and C++?
back: Simula 67

front: What is Pascal (1970) a sucessor to?
back: Algol60. It is conceptually simplified and cleaned up

front: What was the first functional programming language?
back: Lisp (1958)

front: What is significant about Prolog (1972)?
back:
It has an important role in Artifical Intelligence. 
It is intended primarily as a declarative programming language.
Logic is expressed as relations

front: What connection does Simula 67 have to Algol 60?
back: It's an extension of Algol 60 designed for concurrent processes

front: What is the difference between C (1972) and C++ (1980)?
back: C++ is an object-oriented extension of the imperative language C. Designed in 1980.

front: What did algol60 introduce?
back:
• Block Structure
  • Recursion
  • a formal definition

front: Why is cobol still used?
back: For maintenance of legacy business applications.

front: What is the fundamental cycle?
back: Fetch-decode-execute

front: What is the part of the description of the language which seeks to answer the question of what does a correct phrase mean?
back: Semantics

front: What is the part of a language description which asks itself “how do we use a meaningful sentence?”
back: Pragmatics

front: Who developed techniques to describe syntactic phenomena in a formal manner?
back: Noam Chomsky

front: What is a fundamental device for the description of programming languages?
back: context-free grammar

front: What is the aim of lexical analysis?
back: to read the symbols (characters) forming the program sequentially from the input (and generate any errors if needed)

front: What is a very simple operation that scans the input text of the source program from left to right?
back: Lexical analysis

front: What do most official programming language definitions use for their semantic description?
back: natural language

front: Semantics is a relation between what?
back: signs (correct sentences) and meanings

front: What determines the correct sentences with which a recipe is expressed?
back: The syntax

front: What does Chomsky use to limit the ambiguity that is always present in language?
back: formalisms

front: What is a fundamental tool for describing the syntax of programming languages?
back: generative grammar

front: What are Context-Free Grammars?
back: a notation for the concise and precise expression of recursive definitions of strings

front: In the context of programming languages, context-free grammars were used for the first time in the definition of what language?
back: Algol60

front: What is BNF?
back: Backus Naur normal form

front: Is a derivation tree ordered or unordered?
back: Ordered

front: What are nodes without children?
back: leaves
